lead.title: Persistence vs Freshness: Codex and Gemini CLI Tighten Two Different Boundaries for Agent Workflows

articles.title: Persistence vs Freshness: Codex and Gemini CLI Tighten Two Different Boundaries for Agent Workflows
Gemini CLI Turns Issue Triage Into A Caretaker Worker
articles.dek: Gemini CLI added a caretaker triage worker and GitHub egress handler, moving issue sorting toward an agent-run workflow with locks, retries, labels, comments, and human handoff states.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster showing Gemini CLI issue triage becoming a caretaker worker with source cards for triage, locks, egress, and a live-deployment caveat.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: issue triage becomes a worker.

articles.title: API Discovery: RNB Turns Buildings Into Agent-Readable IDs
articles.dek: Compact source brief, not the lead: the RNB building API is narrow but useful because it gives agents a public building identifier before they draft local permit, retrofit, insurance, or asset records.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster showing the RNB building API turning addresses into public building IDs with documentation, endpoint, INSEE, bbox, and rate-limit caveat cards.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: buildings get public IDs.

articles.title: Builder Work: Zerodecimal Makes Vibe Coding Prove Arithmetic
articles.dek: Alexandros Kyriakakis publicly said zerodecimal was built with Claude Code, Ultracode, and Fable 5; the repo is worth studying because the generated arithmetic is surrounded by proof scaffolding.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster showing zerodecimal proof scaffolding around AI-assisted arithmetic with cards for HN proof, div10.go, table tests, allocation gates, and benchmark caveat.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: vibe coded arithmetic needs proofs.

articles.title: Harness Engineering Is Becoming The Field's Self-Improvement Layer
articles.dek: Lilian Weng's July 4 map of self-improving harnesses gives builders a constructive middle path: improve the runtime around the model, but keep evidence, permissions, and evaluation legible enough that the loop can be trusted.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster with the hook 'Not the model. The harness.', five source cards feeding into a circled runtime layer, and a red caveat stamp that says the pattern is not a shared standard yet.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: harness improvement only earns trust when the receipts stay visible.

articles.title: Shepherd Makes Safety Evidence A Release Story
articles.dek: Shepherd's 0.3.0 release commit is a Daily Story because Derek Chong did not only ship features; the public release note makes executed safety evidence part of the project contract.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster showing Shepherd release evidence cards for 0.3.0, safety tests, apply settlement, and a release-evidence caveat.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: releases need receipts.

articles.title: OpenClaw Makes Agent Audit Trails A Runtime Contract
articles.dek: OpenClaw added a metadata-only audit ledger on July 6, giving operators a way to inspect agent run events, native-search outcomes, tool actions, statuses, and errors without opening full transcripts.

illustration.alt: A rough marker diagram titled Audit the run, not the chat, showing native search outcomes and tool actions feeding a metadata-only audit ledger, with evidence cards and a caveat stamp.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: OpenClaw turns agent runtime events into metadata receipts, with limits.

articles.title: API Discovery: data.gouv.fr Turns Dataset Search Into Agent Intake
articles.dek: The data.gouv.fr catalogue API gives agents a public way to search French datasets, inspect metadata, and find attached resources before they act on a claim about public data.

illustration.alt: A rough marker poster telling agents to find the data first, with api.gouv.fr, data.gouv.fr Swagger, dataset, organization, and resource metadata cards feeding a dataset intake conclusion.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: data.gouv.fr turns dataset search into agent intake.

articles.title: Builder Work: Live Log Viewer Maps Parallel Agents
articles.dek: Live Log Viewer reads local Claude Code and Codex transcripts, reconstructs session parentage, and turns parallel agent work into a live map without adding a cloud service or database.

illustration.alt: A rough marker poster titled Agents already left a map, showing Show HN, transcript roots, scanner lineage, a parentage tree, and tmux review controls feeding a circled conclusion.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: Live Log Viewer recovers agent structure from local transcripts.

articles.title: Codex Pins Network Denials To The Exact Exec
articles.dek: Codex's July 6 Linux network-attribution fix turns a vague shared-proxy denial into a traceable event tied to the exact exec call and command that opened the connection.

illustration.alt: A rough marker poster asking which exec touched the network, with Codex PR, active exec registration, Linux sandbox helper, proxy attribution frame, and concurrent test cards feeding a circled denial caller.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: Codex makes Linux network denials name their exec.

articles.title: Hermes Makes Yolo Obey User Deny Rules
articles.dek: Hermes added `approvals.deny` on July 5, giving users glob rules that block terminal commands even when the agent is running in yolo or approvals-off mode.

illustration.alt: Rough zine-style diagram showing Hermes deny rules, yolo bypass ordering, tests, and a reverted plugin gate around a circled user-owned hard stop.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: yolo can move fast, but Hermes now gives users a hard stop.

articles.title: API Discovery: Recherche d'entreprises Turns Company Search Into Agent Triage
articles.dek: API Recherche d'entreprises exposes public French company search through text, SIREN/SIRET, geography, NAF, and employee-size filters.

illustration.alt: Rough zine-style diagram showing API Recherche d'entreprises evidence cards for OpenAPI search, SIREN/SIRET, filters, and rate limit feeding company triage before outreach.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: company search is a compact agent check before outreach.

articles.title: Builder Work: Mycelium Makes Agents Prove The Product Before Coding
articles.dek: Haabe's Mycelium is an open-source Claude Code plugin that was publicly described as built using itself and repo-inspected as a decision framework with receipts, git-stored state, and agent skills.

illustration.alt: Rough zine-style diagram showing Mycelium evidence cards for built using itself, decision scales, YAML in git, and receipts feeding discovery before delivery.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: Mycelium makes the agent earn the build before it codes.

articles.title: OpenWork Turns OpenCode Into A Shareable Agent Host
articles.dek: OpenWork's source trail shows a desktop app, server, orchestrator, router, file sessions, approval flow, export safety, and single-org deployment policy around OpenCode rather than a thin UI wrapper.

illustration.alt: Rough zine-style diagram showing OpenCode inside an OpenWork host boundary with evidence cards for orchestrator, server sessions, file gates, export safety, and single-org policy.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: OpenWork turns OpenCode into a shareable agent host.

articles.title: Pi Turns Issue Analysis Into A Shareable Session Trail
articles.dek: Pi's July 6 commits changed issue analysis from a CI artifact into a staff-triggered, gist-backed session that can be summarized in public and imported back into a local checkout.

illustration.alt: Rough zine-style diagram showing a Pi issue-analysis comment trigger flowing through gist export, session URL, and agent summary into reviewable agent work.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: Pi turns issue analysis into a trail maintainers can inspect.

articles.title: OpenCode Turns OpenAPI Into Agent Tool Contracts
articles.dek: OpenCode merged a CodeMode OpenAPI adapter on July 4, giving hosts a way to turn API specs into model-visible tools while keeping credentials and unsupported operations outside the agent's reach.

illustration.alt: A rough zine-style diagram showing OpenAPI specs turning into agent tool contracts, with host-side auth and skipped operations called out.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: API specs become safer agent tools only when the host keeps the hard boundaries.

articles.title: API Discovery: API Association Turns Nonprofit Identity Into Agent Checks
articles.dek: API Associations exposes French nonprofit identity data from the RNA and Sirene sources, giving agents a public way to verify an association before drafting eligibility, grant, or onboarding work.

illustration.alt: A rough zine poster showing an AI agent checking a French association through RNA and Sirene source cards before drafting.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: nonprofit claims need a register check before the draft.

articles.title: Builder Work: Scopewalker Gives Agents A Complexity Gauge
articles.dek: Timo Haanpaa's Scopewalker MCP is worth studying because an AI-assisted build turns coding standards into read-only measurements an agent can call before it edits.

illustration.alt: A zine-style diagram with gauges for complexity, documentation, code smells, thresholds, and prop drilling feeding an agent before it edits.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: give the agent a gauge before it touches the code.

articles.title: OpenClaw Makes Session Threads A Maintainer Problem
articles.dek: Peter Steinberger's OpenClaw session-management PR turns chat history from a recency list into a lifecycle surface with pinning, archiving, read-only state, and proof screenshots.

illustration.alt: A rough marker poster showing OpenClaw session threads moving through pin, archive, rename, restore, and active-run safeguards.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: durable agent threads need lifecycle traffic rules.

articles.title: OpenWork Makes Team Memory A Governed Agent Surface
articles.dek: OpenWork's July 2 Memory Bank merge turns saved agent context into a user-scoped, searchable capability with tests, schema checks, and explicit no-secrets guidance.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk evidence board showing OpenWork Memory Bank v0 behind user-scope, search-first, MCP execute, and fulltext-index gates.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: memory becomes useful only when scope, search, deletion, and tool boundaries are visible.

articles.title: API Discovery: API Sirene Turns Company Identity Into Agent Checks
articles.dek: INSEE's API Sirene open data gives agents a public way to check French company and establishment identity, with SIREN/SIRET scope and diffusion caveats close to the source.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk API Sirene checklist turning SIREN and SIRET identifiers into company identity checks with quota and diffusion caveats.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: API Sirene turns company identity into an agent check, with limits attached.

articles.title: Builder Work: KISS Sorcar Turns Self-Hosting Into Agent Discipline
articles.dek: Koushik Sen's KISS Sorcar is not interesting because it uses agents; it is interesting because the public repo turns self-hosting pressure into invariants, worktrees, parallel agents, and reproducible checks.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk loop showing KISS Sorcar self-hosting, repo receipts, worktree isolation, parallel agents, and durable invariants.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: self-hosting is a stress test when invariants and worktrees hold the line.

articles.title: Tau Keeps Branch Context For Contributors
articles.dek: A July 3 Tau PR from Rian Dolphin turns branch summaries into a contributor-facing safeguard: the next branch should remember why the previous one existed.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk branch diagram showing Tau session context crossing a branch through Rian Dolphin's PR 227.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: branch summaries become a contributor-facing context bridge.

articles.title: OpenCode Turns Agent Review Into A First-Class Work Surface
articles.dek: A July 2 review-panel overhaul moves OpenCode's agent output closer to a readable control surface for changed files, comments, media, and stability checks.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk illustration showing OpenCode PR 31882, review-panel source, regression tests, and a circled conclusion that review is the control surface.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: review becomes the control surface.

articles.title: API Discovery: API Adresse Turns French Addresses Into Agent Checks
articles.dek: API.gouv's API Adresse page exposes a fully open geocoding source that agents can use to validate French addresses before routing, mapping, or form decisions.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk illustration showing API Adresse turning an address string into labels, scores, postcodes, city codes, and coordinates with a verify-before-action caveat.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: address checks still need human confirmation.

articles.title: Builder Work: 143 Turns Coding Agents Into Team Infrastructure
articles.dek: Assembled's open-source 143 project teaches a practical builder lesson: make agent work reviewable by routing it through durable sessions, sandboxes, previews, and normal pull requests.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk illustration showing 143 turning browser, Slack, and Linear requests into durable coding-agent sessions, sandboxes, and reviewable PR outputs.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: team-owned agent work needs durable review paths.

articles.title: OpenWork Reverts Its Provider Wizard To Hold Dev Stable
articles.dek: Ben's July 1 revert turns a feature rollback into a useful maintainer story: the project tried a provider probe and model picker, then pulled them back before the dev branch carried the risk.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk illustration showing OpenWork provider wizard PRs being rolled back by PR 2420 to hold the dev branch stable.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: branch stability wins the provider-wizard round.

articles.title: OpenClaw Lets Agents Wake After A Command Exits
articles.dek: OpenClaw added an on-exit cron schedule so a gateway-owned watcher can wake an agent after a watched process finishes.

illustration.alt: Rough Diagram Punk poster showing OpenClaw command exit as a gateway-owned trigger with a one-shot caveat.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: OpenClaw moves command-exit wakeups under gateway supervision.

articles.title: API Discovery: API Impot Particulier Turns Tax Proof Into An Agent Check
articles.dek: France's API.gouv catalog exposes a DGFiP tax-data API where agents must treat authorization and proportionality as the product, not an afterthought.

illustration.alt: Rough Diagram Punk poster showing API Impot Particulier with eligibility-first and restricted-access warnings.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: API Impot Particulier starts with authorization, not a test call.

articles.title: Codex Gives Subagents A Delivery Receipt
articles.dek: A Codex commit adds structured send and receive logs for multi-agent communication, making delegation easier to trace without making it today's lead.

illustration.alt: Rough Diagram Punk poster showing Codex subagent send and receive events joined by a communication ID.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: Codex gives subagent handoffs a delivery receipt.

articles.title: Mario Zechner Makes Pi Sessions Render Their Own Evidence
articles.dek: Pi's latest extension change lets durable session entries carry custom rendering, turning a maintainer's minimal-core stance into visible public work.

illustration.alt: Rough Diagram Punk poster showing Pi extensions leaving custom session evidence through entry renderers.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: Pi lets extension-owned session evidence become visible.

articles.title: Hermes Makes Agent Approvals Thread-Local
articles.dek: Hermes replaced a shared approval flag with thread-local state, then paired that control with secret redaction and delegation-budget tests.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster showing Hermes approval commits as source cards feeding into a circled tested runtime boundary, with a caveat stamp about limited security proof.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: approvals became state the runtime has to keep straight.

articles.title: API Discovery: API Entreprise Makes Company Checks A Permissioned Agent Step
articles.dek: API Entreprise gives agents a public map for company checks, but the source also makes the permission boundary impossible to miss.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster showing an API Entreprise request plan stopped at eligibility and token checks before any company-data call.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: plan the company check before touching the permissioned API.

articles.title: Codex Reset The Meter After Background Work Became User Work
articles.dek: OpenAI's public Codex usage-reset note reads like a customer-relief post. The public Codex repository shows something more specific: a cleanup across auto-review prompts, subagent delegation, review-time suggestions, and reset-credit visibility, while the usage backend remains outside the public diff.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster titled Background work has a meter now, with four source cards for auto-review prompt revert, subagent guardrails, review suggestions off, and reset credits UI.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: the Codex reset story was a boundary cleanup, not one bug.

articles.title: OpenCode's Hardening Day Removes Layer Shortcuts
articles.dek: Agent Pulse saw OpenCode as a July 1 hardening signal, and the source receipts point to layer cleanup rather than launch noise.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster showing stacked OpenCode layers with shortcut arrows crossed out and source cards for hardening commits.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: the useful signal was the shortcut being removed.

articles.title: Tau Lets Outside Contributors Shape The Exit Door
articles.dek: Tau's smallest command change became a public contributor story when an outside branch added /exit and the maintainer followed with contributing rules.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster showing a slash-exit command card opening into a contributor doorway, with Tau pull requests and contributing guidelines as source cards.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: a tiny exit alias marked a wider contributor door.

articles.title: OpenClaw Narrows Who Can Wake An Agent
articles.dek: A cluster of OpenClaw commits put scheduled wakes, ACP runtime controls, and browser proxy invocation behind narrower target and scope checks.

illustration.alt: Diagram Punk poster showing OpenClaw commits as source cards narrowing scheduled wake and gateway control paths toward a scoped permission circle.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: OpenClaw turns wake and gateway control into scoped permissions.

articles.title: API Discovery: API Geo Turns French Places Into Agent Inputs

articles.title: Pi Coding Agent You Can Keep Rewriting
articles.dek: The Pi repo shows a different coding-agent bargain: a small terminal harness, a public maintainer culture, and a source-visible insistence that workflows should live in extensions, skills, packages, files, and external sandboxes instead of a swollen core.

articles.title: Agent Control Is Moving Into the Live Interface
articles.dek: Fresh OpenClaw, Crush, Hermes Agent, and Codex commits show approvals, steering, and configuration errors moving from background policy into the moments where a human can actually change an agent run.

illustration.alt: A rough zine-style diagram shows four evidence cards for OpenClaw, Crush, Hermes, and Codex feeding a central running-agent box, with a circled conclusion that human control is moving into the run and a caveat stamp that the commits are not a shared standard.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: the policy matters only if the operator can touch it mid-run.

articles.title: The Twelve-Parent Octopus That Made Git Blink
articles.dek: In January 2006, ACPI maintainer Len Brown folded eleven Linux topic branches into one twelve-parent merge commit. It looked almost comic, pushed Git's own tools to grow sharper eyes, and now reads like a warning label for the agent era: clean integration is not the same as clear accountability.

illustration.alt: A rough zine diagram shows twelve parent lines feeding into Linux commit 9fdb62af above a Len Brown ACPI maintainer desk, then points to a row of agent pull requests with the warning that a clean merge is not clear accountability.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: the old octopus was a graph stunt; the agent-era version is a judgment problem.

articles.title: Agent Identity Is Becoming Runtime Infrastructure
articles.dek: Fresh Hermes Agent, OpenClaw, and Codex commits show profile, credential, and model-choice state moving out of setup screens and into live agent runtime controls.

illustration.alt: Rough zine diagram titled The setting is now part of the run, with evidence cards for Hermes profiles, Hermes sockets, OpenClaw auth SQLite, Hermes credentials, and Codex reasoning efforts feeding into a circled conclusion: Right identity context or wrong action.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: the setting is now part of the run.

articles.title: Git Won Source Control. AI Agents Are Testing What a Commit Means
articles.dek: Linus Torvalds's first eleven-file commit made content-addressed history the durable core of software collaboration. Twenty-one years later, agents are multiplying branches, worktrees, commits, and pull requests - and forcing source management to answer who acted, why, and who is accountable.

illustration.alt: A rough zine diagram connects Git's compact 2005 root-commit record through a forge layer of review and checks to three parallel agent worktrees, while a missing-context strip lists delegation and session details outside the commit.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: Git still records the change; the agent era is forcing accountability into the layers around it.

articles.title: Agent Events Are Getting Return Addresses
articles.dek: Fresh Hermes Agent, Crush, OpenClaw, and Codex commits show agent runtimes attaching correlation, provenance, and client identity to events so the right run, session, surface, or device receives them.

illustration.alt: Rough zine-style Diagram Punk poster titled Every Event Needs a Return Address, with four evidence cards for Hermes Agent correlation IDs, Crush RunID, OpenClaw forwarded-message provenance, and Codex remote client identity feeding into a circled Right Event to Right Owner conclusion.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: right event, right owner.

articles.title: Google's Agent Center of Gravity Has Moved Beyond Gemini CLI
articles.dek: From an Apache-licensed terminal agent to the non-public shared harness behind Antigravity CLI, desktop, SDK, and Managed Agents, Google's I/O transition is an architecture and governance shift, not a rename.

illustration.alt: A rough zine diagram shows the open Gemini CLI source tree feeding toward a central shared Antigravity harness that branches into CLI, desktop, SDK, and Managed Agents, with a caveat stamp that Gemini CLI still ships.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: the terminal proved the agent; Google is consolidating the product around the shared harness.

articles.title: Agent Failure States Are Becoming Instructions
articles.dek: Fresh LangChain, Crush, Gemini CLI, and OpenClaw commits show agent tools turning denial, fallback, routing, and policy drift into explicit next steps.

illustration.alt: Rough zine-style Diagram Punk poster titled Failure Is Becoming a Prompt, with four evidence cards for LangChain, Crush, Gemini CLI, and OpenClaw feeding into a circled Explicit Next State conclusion and a caveat stamp that the commits are independent, not a standard.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: explicit next state beats vague failure.

articles.title: Rust's Bootstrap Chain Is a Trust Story, Not a Smoking Gun
articles.dek: The Rust repo and compiler docs confirm the Trusting Trust shape: modern rustc starts from an earlier rustc binary. The evidence supports an audit question, not an accusation.

illustration.alt: Rough Diagram Punk poster titled The Source Is Not the Whole Chain, showing a Rust source tree, prebuilt beta rustc stage0, stage1 and stage2 compiler boxes, evidence cards, and a red caveat stamp saying it is a risk pattern, not proof of a backdoor.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: source review has to include compiler ancestry.

articles.title: Agent Work Is Getting a Back Button

illustration.alt: Rough zine-style diagram titled Agents Need a Back Button, with four evidence cards for Codex cancelled prompt restore, Hermes undo, Codex parentThreadId, and Hermes archive sessions feeding into a circled recovery-is-runtime-safety conclusion and a caveat stamp that the commits are independent.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: recovery is runtime safety.

articles.title: Agent Policy Is Moving Into Delivered Layers

illustration.alt: Rough zine-style diagram showing Codex config commits and Hermes entitlement commits feeding into a circled policy-needs-receipts conclusion, with a caveat stamp that the commits are independent and not a shared standard.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: the settings screen is only a witness.

articles.title: Agent Tool Menus Are Becoming Runtime Infrastructure
articles.dek: Fresh Codex and Hermes commits show a quieter agent shift: the visible list of tools is becoming a runtime contract about what can be suggested, enabled, discovered, and checked without breaking the session.

illustration.alt: Rough zine-style diagram showing Codex and Hermes evidence cards feeding into a circled availability contract conclusion, with a caveat stamp that the commits are independent and not a shared standard.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: the tool menu is becoming part of the runtime contract.

articles.title: Agent Control Settings Are Becoming Session State
articles.dek: Fresh Codex and Hermes commits show a quieter agent shift: permissions, tool behavior, lineage, approval context, and auth fallbacks are being treated as state that has to survive the next turn.

articles.title: Agent Sandboxes Are Learning Their Cleanup Rules
articles.dek: Fresh Codex and Hermes commits point to a practical agent shift: the local machine is becoming part of the runtime contract, with workspace roots, filesystem denies, Docker persistence, orphan cleanup, and lifecycle commands written down in code.

illustration.alt: A rough Diagram Punk poster titled The agent has a machine now, with Codex and Hermes source cards pointing to a circled Cleanup is part of trust conclusion and a caveat stamp saying independent fixes, not a standard.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: cleanup is part of trust.

articles.title: Agent UI Is Becoming the Control Room
articles.dek: Fresh commits in Codex, Hermes, and Gemini CLI point to a practical agent shift: the human interface is turning into the place where long-running work gets linked, stopped, switched, and steered.

illustration.alt: A rough zine-style Diagram Punk poster titled The agent UI is becoming the control room, with source cards for Codex, Hermes, and Gemini CLI feeding into a circled Supervision needs handles conclusion.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: supervision needs visible handles.

articles.title: Agent Context Becomes Runtime State
articles.dek: Fresh commits in Codex, Gemini CLI, and Hermes point to a quieter agent shift: context is being stored, routed, measured, and traced instead of merely stuffed into prompts.

illustration.alt: A rough zine-style diagram titled Context is a runtime now, with source cards for Codex, Gemini CLI, and Hermes feeding into a circled State greater than Prompt conclusion.
illustration.caption: Diagram Punk: independent commits turn context into runtime state.

articles.title: Agent Reliability Is Moving Into the Test Rig
articles.dek: The newest source-readable trail points away from model mystique and toward the workflows that test, trace, serialize, and maintain agent behavior.

articles.title: Agent Runtimes Are Learning Where to Say No
articles.dek: After shipping more visible control planes, agent projects are now tightening the places where tools, plugins, credentials, chat context, and test runs are allowed to flow.

articles.title: Agent Runtimes Are Learning to Ship Their Control Plane
articles.dek: A set of Codex, Hermes Agent, Gemini CLI, and Crush commits shows agent tools packaging the machinery around the model: shells, platform adapters, context profiles, and skill catalogs.

illustration.alt: Newspaper-style ink illustration of an open shipping case with tagged runtime parts, showing packaged shell, plugin, context, and skills components as inspectable records.
illustration.caption: The Engraver draft: hidden runtime machinery as packaged, inspectable control-plane parts.

articles.title: Agent Runtimes Are Making Their Limits Explicit
articles.dek: Recent Crush and LangChain changes show agent infrastructure turning hidden boundaries - shell permission, context overflow, and model token limits - into runtime contracts that users and developers can see.

articles.title: Agent Goals Are Becoming Runtime State
articles.dek: Recent Codex and Hermes Agent changes show coding agents turning goals from chat commands into durable metadata, resumable session state, and queued work control.

articles.title: Agent Runtimes Are Moving State Out of the Shadows
articles.dek: Recent Codex and Gemini CLI changes show coding agents treating settings, queued input, subagent starts, and terminal streams as explicit runtime state instead of invisible side effects.

articles.title: Agent Runtimes Are Learning to Audit Their Own Tools
articles.dek: Fresh Codex and Gemini CLI changes show agent projects treating tool calls, plugins, MCP servers, and subagents as auditable runtime events instead of invisible helper work.

articles.title: Agent CLIs Are Turning Permissions Into a Conversation
articles.dek: The next shift in terminal agents is not just better tools or tighter sandboxes. It is that permissions are becoming live workflow state: negotiated mid-task, scoped to the action, and remembered with just enough structure to keep work moving.

articles.title: AI Agents Are Turning Compaction Into State Surgery
articles.dek: The new problem is not just squeezing a bloated context window. It is preserving the right tail, marking what was compressed, and making the session recoverable when the cut goes wrong.

articles.title: The Real Agent Feature Is Not Losing the Plot
articles.dek: The next terminal-agent moat is not just better tools or bigger models. It is continuity: whether the system can keep plans, transcripts, and working context coherent as sessions stretch, compress, resume, and hit real-world friction.

articles.title: AI Agent Summaries Are Becoming Infrastructure
articles.dek: The shift is no longer just context compression; summaries are being promoted into durable runtime objects with IDs, policies, UI affordances, and maintenance workflows.

articles.title: The CLI Is Becoming an Agent Workbench
articles.dek: The real shift in terminal agents is not bigger models or flashier demos. It is that planning, task state, plugins, and long-lived runtime context are turning the CLI into a place where work gets organized, not just requested.

articles.title: The Next Agent Battle Isn’t Subagents. It’s Delegation Quality.
articles.dek: Subagents used to be the shiny feature. Now the harder question is whether the runtime can choose the right specialist, hand work off cleanly, recover when the chain gets messy, and prove it made the right call.

articles.title: From Tool Chatter to Chapters: Agent CLIs Are Inventing a Narrative Layer
articles.dek: The interesting shift this week isn’t just that Codex and Gemini CLI can do more. It’s that they’re getting better at explaining themselves while they work —turning raw tool noise into something a human can actually follow.

articles.title: AI Coding Agents Are Turning Compaction Into a Policy Engine
articles.dek: The next contest is not just who gets the biggest context window. It is who decides, with discipline, what survives when the window starts to fill.

articles.title: The Next Agent UX Moat Isn’t Speed. It’s Backpressure.
articles.dek: The hard problem in terminal agents is no longer just getting them to do more. It’s deciding what happens when the human tries to steer while the runtime is already busy.

articles.title: AI Coding Agents Have a New Bottleneck: Keeping Work Intact
articles.dek: The next battle is not just who gets the biggest context window. It is who can keep a task alive when the window trims, the app reloads, the session name drifts, or the agent gets interrupted mid-run.

articles.title: Subagents Aren’t Just Getting Smarter. They’re Getting Contained.
articles.dek: The next terminal-agent upgrade is not more helpers. It’s better walls: isolation, cleanup, bounded autonomy, and fewer chances for delegated work to spill across the room.

articles.title: AI Coding Agents Are Turning Approval Settings Into Operating Modes
articles.dek: What used to look like a boring permissions menu is starting to behave like a gearbox. Across agent CLIs, “plan,” “review,” “yolo,” and model-shaped tool policies are becoming distinct runtime states with different prompts, tools, and output contracts.

articles.title: The CLI Is Quietly Becoming an Agent Router
articles.dek: The next terminal-agent shift is not another flashy planning demo. It is the quieter, harder job of helping remote agents actually connect: across protocols, proxies, auth flows, and messy metadata.

articles.title: Web Fetch Is Emerging as a Security Boundary for AI Agents
articles.dek: A year ago, “web fetch” in an agent might have meant little more than “grab the page and hand the text to the model.” Recent code in OpenClaw, Gemini CLI, and Crush suggests that era is ending. In these projects, the web tool is starting to look like a trust boundary: part scraper, part sanitizer, part permission system, part anti-SSRF guard.

articles.title: The New CLI Moat Isn’t UX. It’s How Agent Skills Get Shipped
articles.dek: This week’s most strategic terminal-agent shift is not another demo flourish. It’s the quiet work of turning capabilities into things that can be packaged, cached, moved, and trusted.

articles.title: Sandboxing Is Starting to Look Like a Runtime Layer for AI Coding Agents
articles.dek: The new contest in coding agents is not just who has the slickest prompt loop. It’s who can let an AI touch your machine without making that feel reckless. This week’s code points to an increasingly architectural answer: move sandboxing closer to the runtime itself.

articles.title: The Next CLI UX Battle Is Agent Forensics
articles.dek: The most important agent upgrade this week is not another tool. It’s the growing ability to reconstruct why an agent touched files, spent tokens, and arrived at a code change in the first place.

articles.title: Subagents Are Getting Job Titles, Badge Checks, and a Manager Chain
articles.dek: The biggest shift in coding agents right now is not that they can spawn helpers. It’s that those helpers are starting to look less like disposable tool calls and more like employees with titles, access badges, and a reporting line.

articles.title: Codex forks it, Gemini threads it: execution context becomes first-class
articles.dek: The next big agent primitive is not another tool call. It’s the bundle of permissions, environment, policy, and wiring that tells an agent how to exist for this turn, in this loop, right now.

articles.title: Parallel agents are getting real addresses
articles.dek: This week’s most important agent upgrade isn’t more raw intelligence. It’s workplace logistics. Gemini CLI is giving parallel sessions separate rooms. Codex is giving subagents names you can actually point at.

articles.title: AI agents are getting better at saying “here’s what I finished”
articles.dek: The real upgrade this week isn’t bigger context or flashier autonomy. It’s something more human: when an agent stalls, waits, delegates, or times out, it’s starting to leave behind a useful trail instead of a shrug.

articles.title: Before the Prompt Lands: Codex and Gemini Turn Hooks Into Agent Control Planes
articles.dek: Terminal agents are growing a new kind of muscle: not just better tools, but better middleware. In both Codex and Gemini CLI, the moment right after you hit Enter is becoming programmable territory.

articles.title: Gemini Lets the Model Schedule Parallel Tools. Codex Makes the Runtime Decide.
articles.dek: Terminal agents are learning the same trick — run more tools at once — but Gemini CLI and OpenAI Codex disagree on who should be in charge of that decision. One pushes dependency control up into the prompt and tool schema. The other keeps it down in runtime metadata and locks.

articles.title: Subagents Grow Up: Gemini Isolates Tool Boundaries While Codex Shares Trust by Default
articles.dek: Two agent platforms shipped subagent changes within hours of each other. One tightened what a child agent can see and use . The other tightened what a child agent can teach the rest of the system to trust . Same category. Very different instinct.

articles.title: OpenViking Turns Agent Memory into a Filesystem — and That Changes the Game
Gemini CLI Turns File Tools into Context Sensors — Right as A2UI Trends
Gemini CLI Built the Ask‑User UI That MCP Elicitation Still Needs
Page Agent’s MacroTool Makes In‑Browser Agents Resilient to Messy Tool Calls
Gemini CLI Makes MCP List-Changed Notifications Resilient
Codex Makes Memories a First-Class Writable Root—and Stops Cleaning Through Symlinks
Gemini CLI tightens MCP tool discovery: debounced list_changed refresh, trailing queue, and resilient retries
Codex Rust CLI Update: Memory Writes Now Fit “workspace-write” and Safer Clears Land in f72ab43
Gemini CLI Makes a “Generalist” Sub-Agent the Default Delegate
Gemini CLI and Codex Level Up MCP Safety and Setup: Trust Prompts vs Auto-Install Skills
Persistence vs Freshness: Codex and Gemini CLI Tighten Two Different Boundaries for Agent Workflows

atlasPages.title: Chapter 1: Before the First Model Request
atlasPages.excerpt: A prompt reaches the model only after the runtime has restored state, resolved permissions, selected tool behavior, run interception hooks, and decided which hidden instructions belong in the request.
atlasPages.question: What has the runtime already decided when the model receives its first request?

atlasPages.title: Chapter 2: The Invisible Prompt
atlasPages.excerpt: The model acts on an assembled viewport containing visible messages, hidden turn context, path-triggered instructions, memory records, tool schemas, permissions, and inherited state.
atlasPages.question: What does the agent know that the visible conversation does not show?

atlasPages.title: Chapter 3: Tool Calls: Contract, Authorization, Execution
atlasPages.excerpt: A tool call crosses independently governed boundaries: schema, registration, policy, approval, execution authority, result handling, and audit.
atlasPages.question: What must happen between a model naming a tool and the world changing?

atlasPages.title: Chapter 4: Loops, Workflows, and Recovery
atlasPages.excerpt: Repeated model-tool interaction is only one control structure. Production runtimes combine probabilistic loops with host-defined workflows, queues, interrupts, terminal states, and recovery operations.
atlasPages.question: Who decides the next step, and what happens when execution must stop or change direction?

atlasPages.title: Chapter 5: Delegation Is a Distributed System
atlasPages.excerpt: A subagent is a remote worker with its own lifecycle, context snapshot, authority, failures, and result stream. Treating delegation as a prompt trick hides the contracts that determine whether the work can be trusted.
atlasPages.question: What contracts keep delegated agent work attributable, bounded, and recoverable?

atlasPages.title: Chapter 6: Memory Is a Typed Storage Contract
atlasPages.excerpt: Calling every retained fact memory obscures incompatible stores and guarantees. Thread history, notes, retrieval indexes, summaries, caches, and archives have different owners, lifetimes, and deletion behavior.
atlasPages.question: Where is agent state written, how is it selected for a turn, and what does forgetting actually remove?

atlasPages.title: Chapter 7: The Approval Trap
atlasPages.excerpt: Approval is useful only when it catches a meaningful change in authority, explains that change, and keeps the decision attached to the work.
atlasPages.question: When does an approval prompt protect the user, and when does it merely train them to click yes?

atlasPages.title: Chapter 8: The Human Control Loop
atlasPages.excerpt: A useful interface exposes active work, gives intervention a defined runtime effect, preserves evidence during correction, and resumes the intended session.
atlasPages.question: Can a person see, stop, correct, and resume agent work without guessing?

atlasPages.title: Chapter 9: Trust Must Make Its Case
atlasPages.excerpt: Trustworthy behavior is not a personality trait or product promise. It is an assurance case built from inspectable policy provenance, durable control state, bounded execution, traceable lineage, recoverable records, and explicit residual risk.
atlasPages.question: What evidence would justify an agent's authority before and after it acts?

atlasPages.title: Chapter 10: The Future Has Three Branches
atlasPages.excerpt: There is no single inevitable agent future. Current source evidence supports three competing scenarios: supervised software organizations, governed capability markets, and managed but less inspectable runtime stacks.
atlasPages.question: Which source-visible future are agent runtimes actually building?
